content.text: The Handoff Problem in GenAI Discovery

Research insights become requirements through a translation process that most delivery teams perform informally and inconsistently. When the translation is implicit, three things go wrong: requirements appear without evidence (untraceable), requirements paraphrase what users said rather than quoting it (losing precision), and researcher inferences are presented as user data (mixing evidence with interpretation). Any of these failures undermines the requirement when it is challenged in sprint planning, architecture review, or acceptance testing.

For GenAI features, the handoff problem is amplified because the most important requirements — guardrails, trust constraints, and accuracy NFRs — are exactly the ones most at risk of being lost in translation. A claims handler's 'I'd never let AI submit without my review' must arrive at the engineering team as a verifiable constraint, not a cultural observation.

attrs.alt: Research-to-requirements translation pipeline: raw data → findings → insights → requirements → acceptance criteria

content.text: The Four-Stage Translation Pipeline

The pipeline from raw research to testable requirements has four explicit stages, each with a distinct artefact and a distinct owner. Collapsing stages — jumping from transcript to requirement — skips the synthesis step and produces requirements that cannot be traced to evidence.
Stage
Artefact
Key discipline
Owner
Raw research
Transcripts, observation notes, survey responses
Faithful recording; no editorialising
Researcher
Findings
Verbatim quotes with frequency, severity, participant IDs, finding type
Separate evidence from inference; no interpretation yet
Researcher
Insights
Pattern syntheses labelled as researcher inference
Label inference clearly; cite the findings that support it
Researcher + Product
Requirements
FR / NFR / CONST with traceability to finding ID and verbatim
Testable; owned; specific
Product Manager
Acceptance criteria
Gherkin-style Given/When/Then linked to eval gate
Verifiable in CI or named review process
Product + Engineering
Dataclasses: Research Handoff Model

handoff = ResearchHandoff(feature_name="FNOL Claims Chatbot")

# ── Finding 1: CRM rekeying pain (Phase 2, 5 of 6 participants) ──────────────
handoff.findings.append(ResearchFinding(
    finding_id="F01",
    phase="Phase 2 – Pain Points",
    verbatim="'I spend 10 minutes just copying information from the email into the CRM fields — it's all there, I just have to retype it.'",
    participant_ids=["P01", "P02", "P03", "P04", "P05"],
    frequency="5 of 6 participants",
    severity=FindingSeverity.HIGH,
    finding_type="pain_point",
    researcher_insight="Manual rekeying is the highest-frequency, highest-severity pain point. AI pre-fill is the obvious intervention, but F03 (trust constraint) limits autonomous write.",
))

# ── Finding 2: Damage category error consequence (Phase 3, 4 of 6) ────────────
handoff.findings.append(ResearchFinding(
    finding_id="F02",
    phase="Phase 3 – Mental Model",
    verbatim="'If the AI gets the damage category wrong, the whole claim goes to the wrong team and it can sit for days before anyone notices.'",
    participant_ids=["P02", "P03", "P04", "P06"],
    frequency="4 of 6 participants",
    severity=FindingSeverity.HIGH,
    finding_type="trust_threshold",
    researcher_insight="Damage category is a high-consequence field. AI error here causes downstream routing failure. Requires mandatory verification UI or high accuracy NFR.",
))

# ── Finding 3: No auto-submit trust constraint (Phase 3, all 6) ──────────────
handoff.findings.append(ResearchFinding(
    finding_id="F03",
    phase="Phase 3 – Mental Model",
    verbatim="'Don't auto-submit anything — I need to review before it's saved.'",
    participant_ids=["P01", "P02", "P03", "P04", "P05", "P06"],
    frequency="6 of 6 participants",
    severity=FindingSeverity.HIGH,
    finding_type="constraint",
    researcher_insight="Universal constraint: no AI action writes to CRM without explicit human confirmation. Non-negotiable scope boundary.",
))

# ── Derived Requirements ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
handoff.requirements.append(DerivedRequirement(
    req_id="FR-01",
    req_type=RequirementType.FUNCTIONAL,
    statement="The system SHALL pre-populate CRM fields from the inbound claim submission text and present them for handler review before any data is written.",
    source_finding_id="F01",
    verbatim_evidence="'I spend 10 minutes just copying information from the email into the CRM fields — it's all there, I just have to retype it.' (P01, 5/6 participants)",
    owner="Product",
    acceptance_criteria=[
        "Given a new FNOL submission, when the AI processes it, then all identified fields are pre-populated in the review UI within 3 seconds",
        "Given the pre-populated fields, when the handler reviews them, then no data is written to CRM until handler explicitly submits",
    ],
))

handoff.requirements.append(DerivedRequirement(
    req_id="NFR-ACC-01",
    req_type=RequirementType.NON_FUNCTIONAL,
    statement="Damage category classification accuracy SHALL be ≥ 96% as measured on the weekly LLM-judge eval set.",
    source_finding_id="F02",
    verbatim_evidence="'If the AI gets the damage category wrong, the whole claim goes to the wrong team and it can sit for days before anyone notices.' (P02, 4/6 participants)",
    owner="AI Quality Team",
    acceptance_criteria=[
        "Given the weekly 200-case eval set, when LLM-judge scores damage category, then accuracy ≥ 96%",
        "Given a deployment where accuracy drops below 96%, then CI gate blocks release",
    ],
    eval_gate="eval_damage_category_accuracy",
))

handoff.requirements.append(DerivedRequirement(
    req_id="CONST-01",
    req_type=RequirementType.CONSTRAINT,
    statement="The system SHALL NOT write any data to the CRM or downstream systems without explicit handler confirmation on each record.",
    source_finding_id="F03",
    verbatim_evidence="'Don't auto-submit anything — I need to review before it's saved.' (P01–P06, 6/6 participants)",
    owner="Engineering + Product",
    acceptance_criteria=[
        "Given any AI-generated field value, when the handler views the pre-fill UI, then a submit action is required before any write occurs",
        "Given a system error during handler review, when the session times out, then no partial data is written",
    ],
))

errors = handoff.validate()
print(errors)  # []

missing = handoff.high_severity_without_requirement()
print([f.finding_id for f in missing])  # []
Finding-Type to Requirement-Type Translation

Each finding type maps to a different requirement category. Getting the mapping right prevents pain points from becoming over-specified functional requirements and trust constraints from being softened into UX guidelines.

# Translation patterns: finding type → requirement type

# PAIN POINT → Functional requirement
# "I have to retype the same data from the email every time"
# → FR: "System SHALL pre-fill CRM fields from submission text"

# TRUST THRESHOLD → Constraint requirement
# "I'd never let AI submit without my review"
# → CONST: "System SHALL NOT write to CRM without explicit handler confirmation"

# QUALITY EXPECTATION → Non-functional requirement (accuracy / latency)
# "If it's wrong more than 2 in 10 times, I'd stop using it"
# → NFR: "Damage category accuracy ≥ 80% on eval set"
# (Note: translate "2 in 10 wrong" → 80% accuracy, not 20% error rate as stated)

# ERROR CONSEQUENCE → NFR severity classification
# "If it gets the damage category wrong, the whole claim routes to the wrong team"
# → NFR: damage category errors are CRITICAL (not MODERATE); CI gate is blocking

# WORKAROUND → Out-of-scope insight
# "I built my own spreadsheet to track which claims are urgent"
# → Insight: urgency triage is a high-value opportunity NOT in current scope
# → Decision required: include in scope or log as future work

# CONSTRAINT FROM MENTAL MODEL → Guardrail requirement
# "There are some things I would never let AI near — decisions about coverage disputes"
# → GUARDRAIL: "Coverage dispute decisions are out of AI scope; system routes to human"
Finding type
Requirement category
Risk if mapped incorrectly
Pain point (process friction)
Functional requirement (what system does)
Under-specification: vague 'system should help with X' without testable behaviour
Trust threshold
Constraint requirement (what system must NOT do)
Softening: converted to 'recommendation' rather than hard constraint
Quality expectation (error tolerance)
Non-functional requirement (accuracy threshold)
Misquantification: '2 in 10 wrong' becomes 80% accuracy target, not 20% error rate
Error consequence severity
NFR severity classification (blocking vs advisory gate)
Under-specification: critical error treated as advisory; regression ships to production
Constraint from mental model
Guardrail / out-of-scope boundary
Scope creep: 'never let AI near coverage disputes' is ignored; feature overscopes
Workaround
Backlog opportunity (not a current-sprint requirement)
Scope inflation: workaround-solving added to sprint before feasibility is assessed
Traceability: Every Requirement Links to a Finding

Traceability is the practice of linking every requirement to a specific research finding and verbatim quote. Without it, requirements cannot be challenged in review, cannot be scoped accurately, and cannot be updated when research evidence is superseded. The `ResearchHandoff.validate()` method above catches the two most common failures: requirements with no traceable finding and high-severity findings with no derived requirement.

# Traceability anti-patterns in GenAI requirements handoff

# ✗ Requirement without a source finding
UNTRACEABLE = {
    "req_id": "NFR-LATENCY-01",
    "statement": "Response time SHALL be ≤ 2 seconds",
    "source_finding_id": None,
    # Problem: no evidence this matters to users; may be over-specified or under-specified
    # Fix: find the interview moment that motivated this threshold, or acknowledge it's
    # an engineering assumption (and label it as such)
}

# ✗ Requirement without a verbatim anchor
NO_VERBATIM = {
    "req_id": "FR-03",
    "statement": "System shall display confidence scores on all outputs",
    "verbatim_evidence": "Users said they wanted more transparency",
    # Problem: paraphrase destroys precision; no way to evaluate whether the requirement
    # matches what the user actually asked for
    # Fix: "P03 said: 'I'd want to know how sure it is — something like a percentage'"
}

# ✗ Finding inferred as requirement without checking feasibility
PREMATURE_INFERENCE = {
    "finding": "P04 said: 'I'd love it if the AI could flag which claims are likely fraudulent'",
    "mistake": "Immediately derived as FR: 'System SHALL flag suspected fraud'",
    # Problem: fraud detection is a separate regulated capability requiring its own
    # feasibility assessment, legal review, and fairness audit
    # Fix: log as out-of-scope opportunity; do not derive as current-sprint requirement
}

# ✗ Researcher inference presented as user data
INFERENCE_AS_DATA = {
    "stated_as_finding":  "Users want AI to take over the full FNOL triage process",
    "actual_evidence":    "P01 said they'd trust AI on routine damage categories; "
                          "P03 said they'd never let AI flag urgency without review",
    # Problem: researcher extrapolated beyond what users said;
    # requirement derived from inference not evidence
    # Fix: label all inferences as 'Researcher synthesis:' in the findings doc
}

The anti-pattern of labelling researcher inference as user data is the most consequential: it produces requirements that appear user-grounded but are actually the researcher's hypothesis. When engineering challenges the requirement, there is no user evidence to cite — only an interpretation that may be wrong.
The Handoff Meeting

The research handoff meeting is a structured 90-minute working session, not a presentation. Its purpose is to walk through every high-severity finding alongside its derived requirement and reach agreement on whether the requirement is correctly scoped, correctly typed, and correctly owned before it enters the product backlog.

The pre-meeting artefact checklist ensures the meeting spends its time on decisions, not discovery. If the findings document, requirements draft, and acceptance criteria draft are not complete before the meeting, reschedule rather than improvise — handoff decisions made without the artefacts produce requirements that need to be re-agreed once the artefacts arrive.
Handling Weak Evidence

Not all findings are equally strong. A constraint mentioned by 6 of 6 participants is a hard requirement. A preference mentioned by 1 of 6 is a hypothesis. The handoff process must distinguish between them and treat them differently.
Evidence strength
Threshold
How to treat in handoff
Strong finding
≥ 4 of 6 participants; or 1 participant with critical severity consequence
Derive requirement; include in current sprint requirements doc
Moderate finding
2–3 of 6 participants; severity medium or below
Log as hypothesis in backlog; validate in Wave 2 research or pilot
Weak finding
1 participant; no corroborating observation data
Log as question; do NOT derive a requirement until validated
Researcher inference only
No participant quote; synthesised from multiple indirect signals
Label explicitly as inference; require stakeholder agreement before deriving requirement

Weak evidence findings should not disappear — they go into an 'open questions log' that drives Wave 2 research or pilot observation. The most impactful features often emerge from 1-of-6 findings that turned out to be representative of a group that was undersampled in Wave 1.
Out-of-Scope Discoveries

Discovery research consistently surfaces valuable opportunities outside the feature's intended scope. These should not be discarded — they should be captured in a structured out-of-scope log that feeds the product roadmap.
Discovery
Why out of scope
Disposition
'I'd love AI to flag likely fraud' (P04)
Fraud detection is a regulated capability requiring separate legal and compliance review
Log as backlog opportunity with feasibility gate
'The handoff between AI and human is confusing — claimants don't know who they're talking to' (P05)
Agent identity is a UX problem separate from FNOL AI functionality
Log as UX discovery; route to CX team
'Urgency triage feels arbitrary' (P03)
Urgency logic is a separate workflow problem not solved by the chatbot feature
Log as process improvement opportunity; route to operations

The out-of-scope log is also a risk management artefact: it demonstrates that the research team identified the opportunity and made a deliberate decision not to include it in current scope — rather than missing it entirely. This matters when stakeholders later ask why a feature was not built.
Requirements Quality Checklist

Before any requirement moves from the research handoff into the product backlog, apply this checklist. A requirement that fails any gate should be returned to the researcher for clarification, not promoted to the backlog with a note.
Check
Question
Failure action
Traceable
Does this requirement link to a finding ID and a verbatim quote?
Return to researcher for traceability annotation
Evidence strength
Is the finding strength ≥ 4/6 or 1/6 with critical consequence?
Demote to hypothesis backlog; schedule Wave 2 validation
Testable
Can this requirement be verified by a test or review process?
Rewrite with specific measurable threshold; assign acceptance criteria
Owned
Is there a named individual accountable for meeting this requirement?
Assign owner before promoting to backlog
Correctly typed
Is this the right requirement category (FR / NFR / CONST)?
Reclassify; check that constraint requirements are not softened to guidance
Inference labelled
If derived from researcher inference, is that labelled in the requirement?
Add inference label; note the gap between evidence and requirement
Summary

The research-to-requirements handoff translates raw interview data into verifiable, owned, traceable requirements through four stages: findings (verbatim, frequency, severity), insights (researcher synthesis labelled as inference), requirements (FR / NFR / CONST linked to finding ID and verbatim), and acceptance criteria (testable conditions linked to eval gates). Every requirement must trace to a finding; every high-severity finding must produce a requirement. The handoff meeting is a working session that walks through each high-severity finding and its derived requirement, resolves ambiguity in typing and ownership, and moves weak evidence to an open questions log rather than the backlog. The out-of-scope log captures opportunities discovered outside the feature boundary, ensuring they feed the roadmap rather than disappearing.
